data.title: Chainlink quarterly unlock completed; FTX sold 6.94 million DRIFT tokens.
data.description:
Headline

▌Chainlink Quarterly Unlock Completed, Approximately 19 Million LINK Flows into the Market

On April 4th, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin's monitoring, the Chainlink token unlock, which occurs every three months, was completed approximately one hour ago. A total of approximately 19 million LINK (approximately $165 million) was unlocked and transferred from three non-circulating supply addresses. On-chain data shows that approximately 14.375 million LINK (approximately $125 million) have been transferred to Binance, and approximately 4.625 million LINK (approximately $40.1 million) have been transferred to the multi-signature address 0xD50...8Af.

It is reported that Chainlink typically unlocks approximately 10 to 20 million LINK tokens from non-circulating supply each quarter. The majority of these flow into Binance, while a smaller portion is transferred to the aforementioned multi-signature address and further used in the LINK staking reward distribution contract to support the distribution of staking user rewards. On April 4th, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin, following the approximately $285 million security incident at Drift Protocol, FTX/Alameda sold 6.94 million DRIFT tokens via Wintertermute, worth approximately $320,000, six hours prior. Data shows that Alameda previously acquired approximately 8.33 million DRIFT tokens through vesting unlocks as an investor, worth approximately $6.22 million at the time; their current value has decreased to approximately $380,000. **Market Updates** As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $66,850.35, a 24-hour change of 0.00%; ETH price is $2,050.14, a 24-hour change of -0.50%; BNB price is $587.85, a 24-hour change of +0.60%; SOL price is $80.09, a 24-hour change of... rgb(0, 176, 80);">+0.70%；

DOGE price is $0.09119, 24-hour change+0.80%；

XRP price is $1.32, 24-hour change+0.10%；

TRX price is $0.3160, 24-hour change+0.30%；

WLFI price is $0.09834, 24-hour change

Blockchain Applications

▌SurfLiquid Launches AI-Powered Stablecoin Savings Vault on the Polygon Chain

On April 4th, Polygon announced on its X platform that SurfLiquid has launched an AI-powered stablecoin savings vault on the Polygon chain. Each user can have their own dedicated non-custodial account and earn returns by holding USDC through the lending market.

▌Chainlink-related addresses transferred 2.5 million LINK tokens to Binance, worth approximately $21.65 million

On April 4th, according to on-chain analyst @ai_9684xtpa, three addresses with non-circulating supply of Chainlink transferred a total of 2.5 million LINK tokens to Binance in the past 4 hours, worth approximately $21.65 million.

▌Data: Crypto Startups Raised Nearly $5 Billion in Q1 2026, Prediction Markets Lead with Over $1.7 Billion

April 4th news, according to DLNews citing DefiLlama data, crypto startups raised nearly $5 billion in the first quarter of 2026, a 16% year-on-year decrease. However, the investment ecosystem continues to evolve, with funds flowing significantly from speculative tokens to practical sectors such as payments and transaction infrastructure. The prediction market sector led with over $1.7 billion, while payments and transaction infrastructure attracted $735 million and $423 million respectively. Top non-crypto institutions such as Sequoia Capital, Founders Fund, Bain Capital, and Alibaba are also accelerating their entry into the market.

The top 10 funding rounds for Q1 are as follows: • Kalshi: $1 billion, valued at $22 billion, led by Coatue Management, a leading prediction market regulated by the CFTC; • Polymarket: $600 million, invested by Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), a leading decentralized prediction market platform; • Rain: $250 million Series C, valued at nearly $2 billion, led by Iconiq Capital, a stablecoin payment infrastructure; • BitGo: $213 million, NYSE IPO, valued at over $2 billion, a crypto custody provider; • Flying Tulip: $206 million, public token sale, led by DeFi architect Andre Cronje, integrating trading/lending/insurance; • Whop: $200 million, Tether. Circle minted another 250 million USDC on the Solana chain 4 hours ago. According to Lookonchain monitoring on April 4th, Circle minted another 250 million USDC on the Solana chain 4 hours ago, bringing the total minted amount in the past month to 10.25 billion USDC. Drift Protocol team-linked wallet deposited 56.25 million DRIFT tokens, worth $2.44 million, to Bybit and Gate. According to Onchain Lens monitoring on April 4th, since the hacking incident, a wallet associated with the Drift Protocol team has deposited 56.25 million DRIFT tokens, worth $2.44 million, to Bybit and Gate. **Important Economic Developments** **Trump: US Warplane Crash Will Not Affect Negotiations with Iran** **On April 4th, according to NBC News, in a brief phone interview, US President Trump stated that the crash of a US warplane would not affect negotiations with Iran.** Trump declined to discuss the specifics of the ongoing search and rescue operation following the crash in Iran, but he expressed displeasure with some reports about the military operation, which he described as clearly a tense and sensitive matter. When asked whether today's events would affect negotiations with Iran, Trump stated that they would not. He stated, "No, absolutely not. No, this is war, we are at war." On April 4th, US President Trump posted: "Happy Easter to all, especially the 186,000 Americans who got private sector jobs in March! My economic policies have created a powerful engine of economic growth, and nothing can slow it down. Factory construction jobs have increased dramatically due to rapid localization and a surge in investment, while the trade deficit has shrunk by 52% in one year!" On April 4th, the Israeli Homeland Defense Command stated early in the morning local time that it had detected missiles launched from Iran towards Israel and was intercepting them. Air raid sirens sounded in several parts of southern Israel. (CCTV News) On April 4, San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly stated in an interview with Fox News that the March unemployment rate falling to 4.3% was "good news," giving policymakers "more time" to balance the risks of both inflation and the labor market. She stated that Friday's jobs report was "really good news" and "very encouraging"; even with various confidence indices remaining low, the data indicated that "the economy and the job market remain stable"; the widening divergence in job growth was also "good news"; the labor market showed no signs of deterioration, which "gave us more time to balance risks, and the current level of monetary policy is just right for this task"; the conflict in the Iranian region could simultaneously affect inflation and economic growth, and the duration of its impact is still difficult to determine; consumers are facing pressure due to rising gasoline prices, which may affect consumer spending; the latest data shows that consumers are still spending, "indicating their confidence in the economy," she said. (Zhitong Finance) She believes that against the backdrop of a policy environment fraught with uncertainty, businesses have become more cautious, hiring intentions are cooling, and companies are increasingly inclined to protect profit margins and respond by increasing productivity rather than expanding employment. "Looking ahead, we expect the labor market to be essentially frozen in 2026, characterized by selective hiring, limited wage growth, and strategic personnel adjustments amid historically tight labor supply." Boussour predicts job growth will be slightly below breakeven, and the unemployment rate will gradually rise to approximately 4.7%. "Given the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, downside risks dominate, and the probability of a recession is 40%," she added. On April 4th, US non-farm payrolls increased by the largest amount since the end of 2024 in March, and the unemployment rate unexpectedly declined. Nick Timiraos, the “new Fed mouthpiece,” points out that this data temporarily alleviates the thorny policy dilemma of “preserving jobs versus controlling inflation.” Interest rate swap markets show that expectations for a rate cut this year have fallen from about 4 basis points before the report's release to almost zero, and bets on a rate cut next year have also narrowed.

▌Federal Reserve's Daly: The Fed should focus on the employment rate, not the number of jobs

On April 4th, Federal Reserve's Daly stated that the economy does not need to create as many jobs as before to maintain the same proportion of the population employed. In this environment, monthly hiring data no longer most accurately reflects the health of the labor market. The unemployment rate may be a better measure.

... She stated, "Ratios and indicators such as the employment-to-population ratio, unemployment rate, turnover rate, or hiring rate can reflect changes in the size of the labor force, thus providing a clearer picture of the health of the labor market." [Golden Encyclopedia] [Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises?] Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise and Bitcoin prices to plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been substantiated by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-prone asset," capable of functioning effectively when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Google's quantum computing paper says Ethereum is dangerous, but the media reports it as Bitcoin.
data.description:
# BITCOIN · # ETHEREUM · # QUANTUM

The white paper specifically points out Ethereum's protocol vulnerabilities, but the headlines are all about "Bitcoin going to zero" —

This isn't a technical issue, it's a traffic issue.

We invited a former Google quantum AI researcher and Bitcoin Core contributor to Palo Alto to dissect this paper and see what the media has missed.

We invited a former Google quantum AI researcher and Bitcoin Core contributor to Palo Alto to analyze the paper and see what the media has missed.

Alan Walker (Silicon Valley investor) ·

Tony Liu (former Google quantum AI researcher) ·

Aaron Chang (Bitcoin Core contributor)

// 4/3/2026 · UNIVERSITY AVE, PALO ALTO · 10:52 AM Tony just came from a group meeting at the Google campus, still wearing his coat.

Aaron stared at his phone screen—a financial media push notification: "Bitcoin 2029 countdown to zero."

Alan glanced at it, pushed his coffee cup to the center of the table, and said: "Fine, you two explain to me clearly today, whether this is really a wolf or not—and where the media lied." Alan Walker: "Tony, give me a basic number first: how many qubits does Google's best quantum chip have right now?" Tony Liu, former Google quantum AI researcher: The Willow chip has 105 physical qubits. The paper itself is of high quality; led by Babbush and Neven, it technically pushes the boundaries of resource estimation. Alan Walker: How many qubits does the white paper say are needed to crack Bitcoin? Tony Liu, former Google quantum AI researcher: At the most optimistic estimate, in a superconducting architecture, at least 500,000 physical qubits are needed. This is under the assumption that the quantum error correction problem has been perfectly solved. Alan Walker: So Google's most advanced chip is nearly 5000 times short of the threshold required by the paper. This is like having a bicycle, publishing a paper saying that if there were a perfect plane that could fly from Beijing to Shanghai in nine minutes, and then the headlines say "Nine minutes from Beijing to Shanghai becomes a reality"—nobody asks where that plane is. Aaron Chang, Bitcoin Core Contributor: And there's another layer: those 500,000 qubits are the number required assuming perfect error correction. Currently, the error rate of superconducting qubits is 10⁻³, and constructing a reliable logic qubit would require approximately 1000 physical qubits for error correction. This engineering problem was not solved in the paper; it was only assumed to have been solved.
105500,000+~4,762×
Google Willow
Actual Number of Quantum Bits
Minimum Physical Quantum Bits Required by the White Paper
Gap Between Reality and Goal

02 The Half the Media Missed: Who Did the White Paper Really Name?

Ethereum — What the White Paper Really Worries AboutBitcoin — What the Media Talks About
→ Block Time 12 Seconds, Extremely Small Attack Window
→ Block Time 10 Minutes, Maximum Attack Window
→ Account Model Public Key Long-Term Exposure
→ P2PKH Addresses Do Not Long-Term Exposure of Public Keys
→ DAS Mechanism Has a Setup Phase Attack Vulnerability
→ Simple Protocol, No Complex Scaling Mechanism
→ High Risk of Privacy Protocol (Tornado Cash)
→ Not Involved in DAS Or privacy agreement risks
→ White Paper Node Name Analysis
→ Relative risk in the paper is lower than Ethereum

03 Bitcoin is not a dead target

Alan Walker:

Even if the hardware gap is real, this gap is narrowing.

Is the Bitcoin community seriously addressing this? Or are they all pretending to be asleep?

Aaron Chang, Bitcoin Core Contributor: It's not that he's pretending to be asleep. NIST officially standardized post-quantum cryptography algorithms—CRYSTALS-Kyber and CRYSTALS-Dilithium—in 2024. A BIP draft for a post-quantum signature scheme is also underway in Bitcoin Core. It's just that there's no buzz, and the media isn't reporting on it. Alan Walker: The truly dangerous scenario is "a quantum computer suddenly reaching its target without anyone noticing." Tony, is this scenario realistic? Tony Liu, former Google quantum AI researcher: It's basically unrealistic. Every breakthrough in quantum computing is highly publicized—papers, press conferences, global media. No organization can quietly build a 500,000-qubit machine without anyone noticing. Signals always precede threats. Alan Walker: This also explains why the paper's author, Justin Drake, said there's "at least a 10% probability of cracking it by 2032," rather than "it's certain to be cracked." The 10% tail risk has been translated by the media as a sure thing, a sign of impending doom. This is information distortion, not a technical conclusion. "My confidence in achieving quantum computing by 2032 has significantly increased, with quantum computers having at least a 10% probability of recovering private keys from leaked public keys." — Justin Drake, Ethereum Foundation, co-author of the white paper, published on the X platform. Is this paper an academic contribution or a strategically designed market event? Tony Liu, former Google quantum AI researcher: I don't question the paper's scientific validity. But one point is noteworthy: They "communicated with the US government" before publication—extremely rare in academic papers. Google itself knows this is more than just an academic paper. Alan Walker: The beneficiary chain is clear: quantum hardware companies, post-quantum cryptography solution providers, migration consulting firms, and Alphabet itself. Reports from stakeholders always tend to amplify threats and lower the bar. This is normal. But you need to be aware of this filter. Aaron Chang, Bitcoin Core Contributor: From the Bitcoin community's perspective, this paper is actually valuable input—it gives us a clearer timeframe to prioritize post-quantum upgrades. Panic will subside, but the technical issues are real, and preparations must be made.

05 Where is the Ethereum Community?

Alan Walker:

I noticed a very absurd detail:

One ​​of the co-authors of the white paper is Justin Drake — a member of the Ethereum Foundation.

He co-authored a paper pointing out that Ethereum has a quantum vulnerability.

What was the Ethereum community's reaction? Basically silent.

The reaction from the outside media was: Bitcoin is finished.

The very information dissemination path is the most absurd part of this farce. Aaron Chang, Bitcoin Core Contributor: There's actually tension within the Ethereum community, it just hasn't been reported to external media. The quantum vulnerability in the DAS mechanism is no small matter; it's a core infrastructure of Ethereum's scaling roadmap, and fixing it would have far-reaching consequences. Ethereum's post-quantum migration is an order of magnitude more complex than Bitcoin's: smart contracts, account abstraction, and various Layer 2 mechanisms all need coordination; this isn't a problem that can be solved by simply changing the signature algorithm. Tony Liu, former Google quantum AI researcher: From an engineering perspective, this is intuitive: the Bitcoin protocol is extremely simple, and the signature-changing algorithm is relatively straightforward. Ethereum is a complex state machine, and post-quantum migration requires the entire ecosystem to upgrade synchronously. These are two engineering problems of completely different difficulty. Alan Walker: So the complete story is this: A researcher at the Ethereum Foundation wrote a paper pointing out that Ethereum's quantum vulnerability is more serious than Bitcoin's. The Ethereum community knows about this and discusses solutions internally. The media glances at it and writes, "Bitcoin is finished." Retail investors panicked and sold off Bitcoin after seeing the media reports. Information is simplified, distorted, and simplified again at every stage of its dissemination. The final push notification that reaches your phone screen has almost no relation to the actual content of that 57-page paper.

06 Are Quantum Computing Stocks Worth Chasing?

Alan Walker:

The last question that investors care about most:

QBTS, IONQ, and RGTI all surged because of this paper.

Is this rally worth chasing?

Tony Liu, former Google quantum AI researcher:

From a technological relevance perspective, these three companies are very different.

IonQ uses the ion trap route, DWave uses annealing, and Rigetti uses superconductivity. The attack model in the white paper is based on a superconducting architecture, making Rigetti the most directly related in terms of technology. However, Rigetti's commercialization progress is the weakest among the three, and its market capitalization is also the smallest. IONQ has the largest market capitalization, but its technological approach is the most indirectly related to this paper. This is a sentiment-driven market, not a fundamental one. Aaron Chang, Bitcoin Core Contributor: I'm more focused on another beneficiary chain: The NIST standardization of post-quantum cryptography has been completed, and now is the eve of large-scale commercial migration. Every large financial institution, every government system, and every cloud service provider needs to conduct post-quantum migration assessments and implementations. This market is far more certain than quantum hardware itself—the window of opportunity is now, not 2030. Alan Walker: My judgment is to completely separate two things: When will quantum computing hardware meet the standards? This is a highly uncertain ten-year question; the short-term gains driven by research papers are likely to revert. Whether post-quantum cryptography migration will occur is a certainty in the present; the standards have been set, and every system needs to be upgraded. The service and software opportunities here are far more substantial than chasing high-flying quantum hardware stocks. Don't confuse "quantum computers are coming" with "post-quantum cryptography migration has begun." These are two different investment propositions with a timescale difference of at least ten years.

Appendix: A Review of Bitcoin's Past "Doomsday Prophecies"

Year

Doomsday Prophecy

Actual Result




2013

Regulation Will Kill Bitcoin

Price × 30

BCH Fork Will Destroy Bitcoin

BTC Dominance Remains Unchanged

2019

Quantum Computers Will Be Cracked Within 5 Years

No Quantum Machine Can Do This

2021

China's Mining Ban Ends Mining

Computing Power Migration, Healthier Network

2022 FTX crash destroys the entire industry Cleanup + regulatory follow-up 2026 Google cracks Bitcoin in nine minutes Google chip has only 105 qubits Dormant addresses from the Satoshi Nakamoto era face greater risks than ordinary addresses when quantum computers truly mature. This is a policy issue that needs serious discussion—but it's entirely different from "the entire Bitcoin market going to zero." Alan's conclusion on 07, turning a 57-page paper into a push notification, is a lie at every step. This Google white paper is serious academic work; the paper team did not lie. But the systemic risk it truly identifies is Ethereum's account model, DAS mechanism, and privacy protocols, not Bitcoin's. Bitcoin, because of its greater fame and higher market capitalization, has become a projection screen for all the panic. The Ethereum Foundation researcher who wrote this paper probably didn't expect the final outcome to be—Bitcoin is finished. More importantly, it's crucial to distinguish between two things: When quantum computing hardware will meet the standards is an uncertain issue with at least a decade to go. The post-quantum cryptography migration has already begun, with standards set to be implemented in 2024—this is ongoing. The former determines when the threat will arrive, the latter determines whether your system is ready. Panic conflates these two events, creating a doomsday narrative that is neither accurate nor actionable. A 57-page academic paper, compressed by the media, becomes a push notification, and then shared on social media, transforms into "Bitcoin will go to zero in 2029." In this chain of dissemination, information is lost at every step, and emotions are amplified at every step. The real risk is never what's written in the paper, but making investment decisions based on a push notification without having read the paper. This article is for discussion and exchange of opinions and does not constitute any investment advice. The characters are fictional, and the dialogue is a literary representation. The technical data is derived from publicly published academic literature (Babbush et al., 2026).

data.title: Bitcoin mining companies flee for the Nth time
data.description:
Author: Zhou, ChainCatcher

Since the end of last year, listed mining companies have launched a wave of collective selling.

Cango sold approximately 60% of its holdings, 4,451 Bitcoins, in February; Bitdeer liquidated its entire Bitcoin inventory in January; Riot Platforms sold 3,778 BTC in the first quarter; and Core Scientific had previously planned to sell approximately 2,500 Bitcoins in the first quarter.

Recently, leading mining company MARA announced that in just three weeks from March 4th to 25th, the company sold 15,133 Bitcoins, cashing out over $1 billion.

Meanwhile, the company announced it would lay off approximately 15% of its employees as part of its strategic transformation into an energy and digital infrastructure company. In fact, miners selling Bitcoin is not a new phenomenon. During the bear markets of 2018 and 2022, mining companies also experienced large-scale liquidation and surrender, leaving behind only the more efficient players. This time, however, the sell-off was triggered not only by the price drop, but also by a new destination—AI data centers. I. Three Motives Behind the Sell-Off On the surface, it appears to be a collective sell-off by mining companies, but upon closer examination, their underlying motives are not uniform and can be roughly divided into three different selling logics. Mining itself has fallen into losses. The first, and most direct, reason is cost pressure. According to CoinShares' latest mining report, the weighted average cash cost for listed mining companies to mine one BTC is approximately $79,995, while the market price of BTC hovers between $68,000 and $70,000. This results in an average loss of nearly $19,000 per BTC, representing an overall loss of approximately 21%. This is no longer just a matter of shrinking profit margins, but rather whether the cash flow can sustain continued mining. The report also shows that the price of computing power once fell to $28 to $30 per hashrate per day in early March, hitting a record low since the halving. At this level, most active mining machines need to keep electricity prices below $0.05 per kilowatt-hour to maintain cash profitability. Currently, about 15% to 20% of the mining machines in the entire network are on the verge of breaking even. Meanwhile, geopolitical tensions in the Middle East are pushing up energy prices, putting continued pressure on electricity costs, an external variable that mining companies have little control over. The QCP Group report points out that with Bitcoin prices significantly lower than average mining costs, mining companies are under considerable pressure, and liquidity has become a priority over hoarding strategies. Against this backdrop, for some mining companies, selling Bitcoin is a practical necessity to maintain operations. AI offers a more stable revenue stream. The second motivation, more strategic, is the most worthy of in-depth analysis in this round of selling. Bloomberg analysis points out that unlike previous sell-offs aimed at covering costs, the funds from this round of selling are being reallocated to the field of artificial intelligence. The underlying business logic is clear: mining revenue is highly dependent on the price of Bitcoin, hash rate difficulty, and electricity prices, making it extremely volatile. In contrast, AI infrastructure is closer to long-term leases, with a CoinShares report indicating profit margins of 80% to 90% and revenue predictability over the long term. More importantly, mining companies already possess readily available resources—cheap electricity contracts, established data centers, sophisticated cooling systems, and mature operation and maintenance teams. Some analysts point out that the construction cost of Bitcoin mining infrastructure is approximately $700,000 to $1 million per megawatt, while AI infrastructure costs as much as $8 million to $15 million per megawatt. This huge cost difference is being monetized on a large scale by mining companies. It is worth noting that behind this transformation stand a group of unexpected driving forces—tech giants and traditional financial institutions. Previously, Google had provided credit backing for its AI cloud platform Fluidstack, disclosing over $5 billion in credit support and guaranteeing AI transformation for mining companies like TeraWulf, Cipher Mining, and Hut 8 in exchange for corresponding equity. Microsoft signed a five-year, $9.7 billion AI cloud service contract with mining company IREN. Morgan Stanley provided Core Scientific with a $500 million loan, with a potential total amount of $1 billion. Their entry provided far more solid capital backing for this transformation of mining companies than imagined. Meanwhile, Core Scientific, TeraWulf, Hut 8, Cipher, and other mining companies have successively signed large AI/HPC contracts, totaling over $70 billion. A CoinShares report mentions that mining companies with AI/HPC contracts have valuation multiples approximately twice that of pure mining companies, indicating that the market is rewarding those companies that have successfully completed the transformation with valuation premiums. Even the most financially stable and least leveraged mining companies, such as HIVE, have proactively scaled back their mining operations and shifted their focus to expanding into AI data centers. This demonstrates that the pressure to transform is no longer exclusive to highly leveraged mining companies, but rather a directional choice faced by the entire industry. Actively Utilizing BTC as a Financial Tool The third logic is relatively astute and the most proactive. Some mining companies choose to sell BTC not out of operational pressure, but as a tool to optimize their balance sheets, such as MARA. Specifically, they use the proceeds to repurchase previously issued convertible bonds at a discount below par value, thus reducing debt and mitigating potential equity dilution risks. For these mining companies, the role of BTC on their balance sheets has quietly shifted from a long-term holding symbolizing faith to a strategic asset that can be flexibly allocated. Furthermore, this round of selling has also seen a relatively rare type of seller: sovereign states. On-chain data shows that the Bhutanese royal government's BTC holdings have decreased by approximately 66% from their peak at the end of 2024, with single transactions in March reaching $35 million to $45 million, and the pace of selling continuing to accelerate. Unlike most countries that accumulate BTC through market purchases, Bhutan's holdings originate from its domestic hydropower mining operations, and this large-scale reduction may be related to the funding needs of its national development projects. This is also one of the largest government Bitcoin divestments on record. The market is facing structural supply pressures from multiple directions and of varying natures due to three overlapping factors: mining losses, AI transformation, debt optimization, and sovereign-level selling pressure. Mining companies' Bitcoin faith is being reshaped by more realistic business logic. II. After Exiting, Each Goes Their Own Way Of course, selling does not equal liquidation. The remaining holdings and subsequent strategies of various mining companies are showing stark divergence. Three Paths, Three Choices The first path is to persist in mining. Represented by CleanSpark and HIVE. Instead of chasing the AI ​​transformation narrative and accumulating debt, CleanSpark relies on a combination of low electricity prices, next-generation mining machines, and low leverage to emerge victorious during the industry shakeout. Their logic is that as high-cost capacity gradually exits the market, the revenue per unit of computing power for surviving mining companies will increase. CleanSpark has publicly stated that at current computing power price levels, continuing large-scale investment in Bitcoin mining is "economically unreasonable," but the company still chooses to stick to its core business, betting that the cycle will eventually reverse. Renowned crypto KOL Blue Fox points out that historically, almost every halving has resulted in miners surrendering, while those who remain are often the more efficient players, gaining a larger share in the next rebound. For these mining companies, sticking to mining isn't stubbornness, but rather a trust in cyclical patterns. The second path is a two-pronged approach. Represented by companies like MARA, IREN, and Riot, these companies maintain a significant BTC holding while simultaneously investing in AI/HPC, using the relatively stable revenue from AI to hedge against the cyclical fluctuations in mining revenue. Essentially, these companies are tackling an asset allocation problem; the answer varies from company to company, but the core logic is that the two business lines support each other, diversifying risk. The third path is a full shift to AI. Represented by companies like Core Scientific, TeraWulf, and Cipher, BTC holdings have relegated them to a secondary position as core assets, with mining gradually becoming a subsidiary of their data center business. CoinShares predicts that by the end of 2026, AI revenue may account for as much as 70% of some mining companies' total revenue, while mining revenue may decline from approximately 85% in early 2025 to less than 20%. These companies will still be mining companies in name, but in reality, they are becoming AI infrastructure operators that started with mining. The potential risk of this path is that the asset-heavy transformation means a huge debt burden, and if AI demand cools down, both ends of the business will be under pressure. Some argue that Google's credit guarantee structure through Fluidstack actually creates a highly concentrated counterparty risk—the entire cash flow chain relies on Fluidstack as an intermediary, and this structure will become a single point of failure should the AI ​​lease market undergo significant changes. Regardless of the path chosen, the ultimate outcome points to the same variable: the price trend of BTC. CoinShares presents three scenarios: ● If BTC recovers to $100,000 by the end of 2026, the hashrate price will rise to approximately $37/PH/day, mining profits will recover, and overall industry pressure will ease; ● If it remains below $80,000, high-cost miners will be forced out of the market at an accelerated pace, and the traditional model of mining, hoarding, and waiting for a bull market will become increasingly unsustainable; ● If it breaks through historical highs, the hashrate price may surge to $59/PH/day, and the industry will enter a new expansion cycle. In conclusion, mining companies face only two possible outcomes: either the price of Bitcoin recovers, allowing them to return to their core business, and the current situation is merely a cyclical historical footnote; or prices remain low, with more and more mining companies transforming into AI data centers, and the company model of mining, hoarding coins, and waiting for a bull market gradually becoming rare in this industry. However, this transformation raises a question beyond its business logic. Mining companies are not ordinary publicly listed companies; their continuous investment in computing power is itself part of the security budget for the Bitcoin network. Sazmining CEO Kent Halliburton once bluntly stated that these companies "hold power contracts, land, and infrastructure, yet they hand over these resources to Microsoft and Google in exchange for rent checks, transforming from protecting the Bitcoin network to storing rack space for hyperscale cloud service providers." When mining no longer generates sufficient economic returns, a rational business decision would naturally be to shift resources; however, if this trend continues, the question of who will bear the long-term costs of maintaining the Bitcoin network will become an issue that cannot be ignored. History may have provided an answer to this question. The Bitcoin network has experienced several large-scale miner exodus, and each time it has operated with greater efficiency. But this time, the miners who left did more than just shut down their machines. Times have changed.

data.title: Anthropic's valuation and investment value
data.description:
These past two days, the market has been abuzz with discussions about a batch of high-tech companies that may go public this year, including OpenAI and Anthropic.

These two companies have been shrouded in mystery and controversy recently, not only because they are direct competitors, but also because there have been numerous rumors surrounding their potential IPOs.

For example, just two days ago (March 31st), OpenAI announced the completion of another $122 billion in financing. This funding round has boosted its valuation to $852 billion. In contrast, Anthropic's valuation after its previous funding round was only around $380 billion, less than half of OpenAI's latest valuation. While OpenAI is extremely popular in the primary market, rumors suggest that its private equity investment in the secondary market has unexpectedly cooled. Approximately 600 million institutions are reportedly in a hurry to divest their $600 million secondary market share, but no one is willing to take it over. However, on the other hand, approximately $2 billion in funds are hoping to enter Anthropic, which has led to secondary market prices for Anthropic exceeding $600 billion. In previous articles, I publicly expressed my optimism about two AI companies that I could somewhat understand, and one of them is Anthropic. If OpenAI and Anthropic can really go public this year, I think the opportunities they present are worth paying close attention to. First, the US stock market is currently experiencing significant volatility; the Nasdaq and S&P 500 have both retreated by about 10% from their highs. If the situation in the Middle East continues to escalate for a while, further driving down US stock prices, that would be even better. Secondly, besides these two AI companies, even larger giants like SpaceX and a host of other prominent companies are waiting to go public this year. This scale of fundraising will significantly drain overall market liquidity. In this environment, the listing prices of OpenAI and Anthropic may not be as high as previously estimated. Finally, between OpenAI and Anthropic, I very much hope that OpenAI can be the first to go public, thus further draining the liquidity of the AI ​​sector, so that it's Anthropic's turn. Once it goes public, its price is unlikely to be too high. These are all qualitative analyses; next, let's try a quantitative analysis of Anthropic. Currently, Anthropic's revenue from enterprise-side (to B) business accounts for more than 80% of its total revenue, making enterprise applications its primary source of income. The US and China differ. In China, enterprise users have a weaker willingness to pay, but in the US, if a product is good, enterprises have a strong willingness and ability to pay. Furthermore, once enterprise users become accustomed to a product, they generally do not easily switch. The reason why some well-known, fossil-level technology companies (such as IBM) continue to exist today is mainly due to their enterprise users. Furthermore, the enterprise user market is generally not easy for foreign competitors to enter, and Anthropic itself is already a leading player in this field. Therefore, once it achieved a monopoly in the US market, the barrier to entry was quite high. Therefore, I am quite optimistic about the Anthropic pattern. Anthropic has been operating at a loss for several years, but based on its current growth momentum, it is estimated that it will achieve initial break-even in about two years (2028) and net profit of around $50 billion by 2030. Starting from 2030, if the company can continue operating for another 20 years, its total net profit will be one trillion US dollars. Based on this rather rough estimate, I think Anthropic's market capitalization is fairly high if it's below one trillion US dollars.

Musk intends to leverage his massive fan base and unwavering faith to create extreme FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) sentiment in the early stages of the IPO, transforming SpaceX into the largest "faith stock" in history. Once this is clarified, Musk's path to wealth accumulation becomes very clear. data.title: The focus of AI payment is not the payment itself.
data.description: "Please reorder the milk tea I bought from that shop yesterday." If one day you could actually say this to your phone, and it would find the merchant, confirm the product, place the order, pay, and arrange delivery, you probably wouldn't first think of a payment revolution. You'd likely just feel that your phone is finally starting to act as a capable assistant. This might be a more appropriate starting point for our discussion of AI payments today. Most people's understanding of AI payments isn't about giving AI a wallet, nor is it about letting a robot decide how to spend money. What people truly experience is whether a single sentence can directly become a transaction. You state your needs, and the system handles the rest. Therefore, what's truly worth discussing about AI payments isn't often the word "payment," as that word is too abstract and distant. For most internet users, the general feeling is: an app or AI assistant can handle their shopping needs directly when they express them in plain language. If we only look at the Chinese market, AI payment isn't actually that unfamiliar. The reason is simple. Chinese users already live in a very mature mobile internet ecosystem: food delivery, e-commerce, maps, ride-hailing, local services, mobile payment, and instant delivery are all interconnected. Often, what users want isn't a completely new payment method, but rather to avoid having to switch between apps and repeatedly click the same buttons. In China, AI payment is more like an upgrade to the e-commerce shopping experience than a complete reconstruction of the payment system from scratch. Alibaba's upgrade of the Qwen App in January this year is a typical example of this direction. According to the official introduction, Qwen has integrated with services within the Alibaba ecosystem, including Taobao, Taobao Flash Sale, Alipay, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps. Users can order food, complete payments in chat, and plan and book trips with just a sentence. It seems like AI has become smarter, but more accurately, it's the platform's existing capabilities that have been reorganized. This is why AI payment is most easily implemented in China not in grand visions, but in very everyday scenarios. For example, "Buy another box of the printer paper I bought last week and deliver it to my office." Or, "Order another delivery from that restaurant yesterday and have it delivered to my home." Or, "Book a restaurant close to the office, for four people, with a budget under 500 yuan." Users understand these commands immediately. Because the AI ​​here isn't some mysterious new financial tool; it's simply a smarter shopping assistant. What the people need, the endlessly competitive Chinese internet giants will do. Today, there are roughly three paths to AI-powered payments. One is to let AI watch the screen and operate on your behalf, clicking buttons, filling in information, and submitting the process like a human. The advantage of this approach is that it can be tested anywhere, but the disadvantages are also obvious: problems easily arise when the page changes, and you often still need to confirm the key parts yourself. For example, the Doubao phone, which was immediately attacked by WeChat upon its release. Another approach is for the platform to simply hand over its service capabilities directly to AI. For Chinese giants, this path is actually the smoothest. Because payment, merchants, maps, orders, and delivery are already under their control. AI doesn't need to imitate the user clicking around; it can directly access the capabilities within the system. In terms of user experience, it's "I said one thing, and it actually did it for me." For example, Alibaba's Qianwen and ByteDance's Doubao. The third approach is even more difficult, involving cross-platform, cross-website, and cross-company collaborations. At this stage, the issue isn't just about product usability, but whether others are willing to let your AI represent users in this process. Currently, we haven't seen any Chinese internet companies attempting this hard-core approach. Today, China's greatest advantage clearly lies in the first two approaches, especially the second. This is why, for Chinese users, AI payment may not seem groundbreaking; their overall feeling is likely not "AI payment is here," but rather "mobile shopping is more convenient." However, once the perspective shifts overseas, things become somewhat different. While e-commerce, food delivery, maps, and payments also exist overseas, they lack the highly integrated platform structure found in China. Service entry points, merchant systems, payment tools, and fulfillment networks are often controlled by different companies. Therefore, when AI attempts to complete a transaction on behalf of a user, the challenge isn't just "can the order be placed?", but "will others accept you as an AI agent?" This explains why AI payments overseas have become so complex. A prime example is the conflict between Amazon and Perplexity. Perplexity's Comet browser touted AI shopping as a key selling point, stating in its official description that it can help users compare products, read reviews, and proceed all the way to checkout. This aligns with many people's most intuitive imagination of AI shopping: instead of browsing websites one by one, AI will pick and buy for you. However, this is precisely where the problem lies. According to court documents dated March 9, 2026, a core argument Amazon made in its lawsuit was that Perplexity's AI agent accessed user accounts and attempted to perform actions "with the user's consent, but without Amazon's consent." Amazon's reasons for disagreeing are largely the same as WeChat's reasons for disagreeing with Doubao Mobile: authorizing AI doesn't mean the website also authorizes it. Your willingness to let it buy things for you doesn't mean the platform is willing to allow a third-party agent into its system to initiate transactions on your behalf. While the stated reason is for customer privacy and security, the core concern is that AI shopping will directly divert billions of dollars of advertising revenue from its sellers. After all, AI shopping is straightforward; it goes straight for the product and doesn't care about ads. In China, these kinds of conflicts are often resolved by the platform's integrated capabilities; however, overseas, once AI needs to complete transactions across websites, merchants, and payment networks, these issues become unavoidable. Therefore, overseas, the primary question for AI payments isn't "how easy it is to use," but rather "whether this can be completed in a closed-loop end-to-end manner." This is why Google, Stripe, and Coinbase have recently been focusing their efforts on this area. They all see the same problem: in the past, internet transactions assumed that the order was placed by a human, who was sitting in front of the screen, browsing, confirming, and paying. But once the order is placed by an authorized AI, that assumption no longer holds true. How does the website know that this AI was truly sent by them? How can the merchant confirm that it hasn't made any unauthorized purchases? How should payment institutions determine whether or not to release the funds? Google aims to first resolve the fundamental trust issue. Launching AP2 in September 2025, it wasn't about teaching AI how to make payments, but rather about answering the fundamental question: when AI initiates a payment on behalf of a user, what makes the system believe it's genuine? Google's approach is to add a verifiable "authorization certificate" to these transactions. AP2 incorporates two crucial elements: a Cart Mandate, which can be understood as the user's signature confirming the specific purchase; and a Payment Mandate, shown to the payment network and card issuer, informing them that the transaction was initiated by an agent, whether the user was present, and the transaction's context. Google later integrated this system with PayPal, creating a more complete merchant solution: merchants can use their own conversational shopping assistant to serve users, and at the payment stage, PayPal Agent takes over, using AP2 to complete the authorization and payment process. Ultimately, Google's approach addresses the fundamental question of "why trust AI to place orders." Stripe's approach is closer to merchants and platforms. It actually involves two layers. The first layer is the ACP, or Agentic Commerce Protocol, launched in September 2025 with OpenAI. You can think of it as a "standard for AI-readable checkout." Stripe doesn't want AI to mimic human actions like clicking web pages and filling out forms; instead, it wants merchants to proactively open up their capabilities regarding products, inventory, and checkout, allowing AI to initiate purchases using a standardized interface. The advantage of this approach is that merchants remain primarily responsible for orders and fulfillment; product display, order processing, and risk control assessments are still under their control, but they don't need to rebuild their systems for each AI platform individually. Stripe later launched the Agentic Commerce Suite, making catalog access, checkout, and payment a one-stop tool to lower the barrier to entry for merchants. However, Stripe also saw a completely different scenario. It's not "AI helping people buy things," but rather "software paying for software directly." Therefore, in March 2026, it launched Machine Payments. In this scenario, AI doesn't buy a Coke, but rather APIs, data, computing power, content access rights, or even pay-per-use services. Stripe's solution is that merchants can directly convert their interfaces to pay-per-use rates, as low as 0.01 USDC, with the money going into Stripe's account, and final settlement and reconciliation still done in Stripe's familiar way. For agents, there's no need to register an account, apply for an API key, or go through a bunch of manual processes; they can make payments while making inquiries. In other words, Stripe is building both an AI shopping checkout system and a micro-payment track between machines. Coinbase takes a different approach. It's more like assuming that in the future, many AIs won't be "shopping assistants" but rather "software with wallets." Therefore, in February 2026, it launched Agentic Wallets. The core isn't to make AI better at browsing websites, but to give AI a real wallet that can spend, receive, and transact, and this wallet has security boundaries. Coinbase's system includes the wallet infrastructure itself, the x402 machine payment protocol, and pre-packaged capabilities such as deposits, transfers, transactions, and earnings management. Its main focus is on solving a different type of problem: if an AI wants to buy computing power, data, pay API fees, or complete some kind of automated transaction on-chain, can it avoid waiting for confirmation every time? Coinbase's official documentation calls this agent an "independent economic agent." This term is a bit strong, but the meaning is clear: what Coinbase wants to do is not to make AI more like a shopping assistant, but to make AI itself a software entity that can handle income, expenditure, settlement, and transactions. If you look at these three paths together, the differences become clear. Google wants to first solve "how do you prove that this transaction was truly authorized by me?" Stripe is more concerned with "how merchants can connect their products and checkout capabilities to AI." Coinbase, on the other hand, has taken a step further to solve "if the payer is software, how does it complete income and expenditure on its own?" This is precisely why, while they all appear to be working on AI payments, their focuses differ. Google is more like adding a layer of trust to cross-platform intermediary transactions; Stripe aims to integrate AI shopping and AI-driven service calls into existing business networks; Coinbase, on the other hand, is betting on a world where software directly pays for software. At this point, the difference becomes clear. In China, AI payments are more like a natural extension of a mature internet system. Platforms have already organized merchants, payments, delivery, maps, and local services; AI's role is to rearrange these capabilities, allowing users to go from "operating themselves" to "simply speaking." Overseas, AI payments are more like a belated catch-up. Because no platform naturally controls the entire transaction chain, once AI is to actually complete transactions for users, it immediately encounters real-world problems such as whether websites will approve it, whether merchants will accept it, whether payment institutions will recognize it, and who will be responsible if problems arise. In China, it's more like pushing the online shopping experience a step further. Overseas, it's more like renegotiating the rules for participation by multiple parties in internet software and online consumption scenarios. This is why Chinese users' first reaction to AI payments might be, "This function is quite convenient"; while overseas markets' perception of AI payments is more like, "The original online transaction methods may need to be changed." Returning to the initial scenario... "Please place another order for the milk tea I bought yesterday and deliver it to my door." The truly interesting part of this sentence isn't the word "payment," but rather how it delegates many tasks that you would normally have to do manually to the system. It needs to know which store you mentioned, which item, its specifications, delivery location, and payment method. Payment is important, of course, but it's just the least significant step in the whole process. Therefore, the focus of AI payment isn't the payment itself. What people really care about isn't which protocol the backend uses, whether it's on-chain settlement, whether it uses stablecoins, or whether it uses encryption algorithms. Ultimately, people will use a very simple standard to judge its usefulness: I told the AI ​​something, can it actually get it done for me?

data.title: Tokens become "money-devouring beasts," and the computing power of major companies has skyrocketed.
data.description:
Have you noticed how quickly the AI ​​industry has changed in the last six months? This time last year, everyone was celebrating the price reduction of computing power. Alibaba Cloud led the charge, announcing "up to 60% price cuts," and Tencent Cloud, Huawei Cloud, and Baidu Cloud all followed suit—it was like a Double Eleven shopping festival. In startup groups, people were constantly showing off their bills: "Look, I only spent a few cents on this million in tokens!" Back then, everyone thought the spring of AI startups had arrived; computing power was as cheap as cabbage, who couldn't afford large-scale model applications? And what happened? The reality check came incredibly quickly. Just last month, the winds completely reversed. Google, Amazon, Tencent, Alibaba, and Baidu all issued price increase announcements within 10 days. How much? Generally, 30% to 50%. The most drastic increase was by Tencent Cloud, with one core product seeing a direct price hike of 400%. From "fire sale" to "rocket-like price hikes," less than a year has passed. What exactly happened? Who is behind the scenes pushing prices up? More importantly, in this wave of price increases, who suffered the most, and who is secretly laughing? Last year they were offering "huge discounts," so why the collective price increases this year? Let's briefly review this dramatic reversal. In April 2025, Alibaba Cloud dropped a bombshell: core products saw price reductions of up to 60%. This wasn't a small-scale operation; it was a genuine "half-price cut followed by a discount." JD Cloud immediately followed suit, saying, "Lower your prices as much as you want, I'll follow," and Tencent Cloud, Huawei Cloud, and Baidu Cloud all followed suit. Suddenly, the computing power market was ablaze with competition, and the price war was fierce. What were the slogans back then? "Make AI affordable" and "Make computing power accessible to all." Many startups actually believed them and started burning through tokens and running models on a large scale. However, a free lunch never lasts long. In January 2026, Amazon AWS quietly did something—without any press conference or prior notice, it directly raised the price of its EC2 servers by approximately 15%. Don't underestimate the seemingly small increase; it's significant: this is the first price hike in the cloud services industry in nearly two decades. Keep in mind that in the past two decades, AWS has lowered prices over a hundred times, always going down, never up. This is like toppling a domino effect.

3Month11On Japan, Tencent Cloud followed up, and its Tencent HY2.0 Instructmodel input price started from 0.0008Yuan/ThousandstokensRising to0.004505yuan/Thousandstokens - an increase of 463%, more than four times. On March 18th, Alibaba Cloud announced a price increase for its computing power card products, from 5% to 34%. Baidu AI Cloud followed suit, increasing its price from 5% to 30%. Those large models that were previously available for free in public beta, such as GLM 5, MiniMax 2.5, and Kimi 2.5, have all ended their "free trial" period and moved to official billing. From "rushing to lower prices" to "rushing to raise prices," why has the change been so rapid? On the surface, it seems cloud vendors are struggling to cope. GPU chips are becoming increasingly expensive, and electricity costs for data centers account for 40% to 60% of operating costs. Furthermore, memory chip prices also began to rise in the second half of 2025, putting real pressure on costs. But what truly made the price increase "inevitable" was another, more fundamental reason—computing power was genuinely insufficient. Who's frantically "eating" tokens? The truth behind 140 trillion. Wasn't there supposed to be a surplus of computing power before? How come it's suddenly insufficient? The answer is: Token was "eaten" too quickly. According to data disclosed by Liu Liehong, Director of the National Bureau of Data Science and Technology, in March of this year: By March 2026, the daily number of token calls in China had exceeded 140. trillions. How staggering is this number? Here are two points for reference: At the beginning of 2024, this number was only 100 billion. In two years, it increased more than a thousandfold.

· 2025At the end of the year, the number is100Trillion. In other words, in just three months, it has increased by another 40%—the increase in just these three months (40 trillion yuan) is 400 times that of the entire day at the beginning of 2024. This is not linear growth; it's a tsunami. So the question is: Who is consuming tokens like crazy? The answer is one word: Agent. Starting last year, products represented by the open-source AI agent OpenClaw (affectionately known as "Lobster") have become incredibly popular. AI has evolved from a robot that can only "chat" into a helper that can "get things done"—booking flights, writing code, creating PPTs, analyzing data… Sounds pretty cool, right? However, the cost is that for an intelligent agent to perform a simple task, the amount of tokens consumed is 10 to 100 times that of a normal conversation. For example, having an AI write a web crawler script for you. In a normal conversation, it gives you a piece of code, you copy it and leave, consuming a few hundred tokens. But if it's an intelligent agent, it has to run the code itself, report errors, debug, run it again, debug again... going back and forth dozens of times, consuming tens of thousands of tokens. Not to mention video generation, which is a "money-devouring beast." Analysis indicates that generating a 1-minute video would consume approximately 10 trillion tokens. However, current video models charge only a few cents to a few dollars for generating a 5-second video—this isn't making money; it's clearly losing money to gain publicity. But the sheer number of users is overwhelming; videos, music, code, data analysis… every field is frantically “consuming” tokens. Supply can't keep up with demand, so computing power naturally goes up in price. This isn't a conspiracy; it's a blatant supply-demand imbalance. Computing Power Ranking Competition: Big Companies Eat the Meat, Small Players Can't Even Get a Sip. The price increase means completely different things to different people. For cloud vendors, price increases are actually a good thing. A brokerage firm has calculated that for every 1% price increase by Alibaba Cloud, its profit margin increases by 1 percentage point. Therefore, the data you see shows that Alibaba Cloud's market share has increased rather than decreased, now accounting for 36% of China's AI cloud market. In the more specific segment of AI usage, Volcano Engine (owned by ByteDance) accounts for nearly 50%—meaning that half of all token usage in China goes through Volcano Engine's pipeline. Meanwhile, the market share of Huawei Cloud and Tencent Cloud is slightly declining. The head effect is becoming increasingly apparent: large companies are getting stronger, and resources are becoming more concentrated. Who's suffering the most? Small and medium-sized AI startups, and those newcomers to the field. The reason is simple: the price increase directly drives up their operating costs. Previously, when tokens were cheap, you could run experiments and adjust models freely without spending much money. Now, with prices several times or even ten times higher, every round of training and every inference requires careful consideration. What's more troublesome is that smaller players have no bargaining power. Large clients can sign long-term agreements with cloud providers, locking in a relatively favorable price. As a startup, spending only tens of thousands of yuan a year on computing power, who's going to negotiate a discount with you? You can only obediently pay the increased price. Many projects that originally planned to develop AI applications quietly shelved them after doing the math. Others that were already in progress either scaled back or stubbornly borne the losses. And the terminal market is fiercely competitive; you can't easily raise prices for users—the competitors are still offering it for free, and if you start charging, all your users will leave. In the end, you have to swallow all the cost pressure yourself. An industry insider complained to me: "I used to think that cheap computing power meant a lower barrier to entry for startups. Now I realize that the barrier isn't lower; it's that they let you in first, then shut the door on you." This is actually a brutal ranking competition. For the past twenty years, cloud vendors have thrived on a strategy of "low prices for volume, securing market share first, then profiting." But that era is over. Computing power has officially bid farewell to the subsidy period and entered the commercial pricing stage. The future competition will no longer be about who is cheaper, but about whose service is more stable, whose ecosystem is more complete, and who can truly help enterprises make the best use of every bit of computing power. In this ranking competition, smaller players are likely to be left behind. Looking back at the rollercoaster ride of the past year, you'll find a rather sobering truth: the shift in computing power from "dirt cheap" to "rocket-priced" is essentially a microcosm of the AI ​​industry's transition from unregulated growth to maturity. The era of free services is over; value-based competition has begun. Business models that rely on subsidies will die out, while products with genuine technology, real-world applications, and a user base will survive, and even thrive, in an environment of rising computing power costs. The core competitiveness of AI startups has never been about how cheap computing power is, but rather what you do with that computing power. In the computing power era, tokens are indeed expensive. But more valuable than a token is a mind that knows how to use tokens effectively.

data.title: Trump's son has been exposed for profiting from the war, and his family is suspected of war-related corruption.
data.description:
Powerus, a drone company partially owned by Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump, Trump's two sons, is selling drone interceptors to Gulf states that are facing attacks from Iran and rely on the protection of the U.S. military.

According to an Associated Press report on April 3, Powerus co-founder Brett Velicovich told the Associated Press that the company is marketing its drone interceptors to some Gulf states, conducting drone interception demonstrations to show how Powerus' defensive drone interceptors can help these countries defend against Iranian attacks.

"Our team is currently demonstrating the interceptor across the Middle East," said Veljkovic. "We have very advanced technology that can save lives." He did not disclose further details regarding which countries Powerus is marketing to. Former White House ethics lawyer Richard Payne, speaking under the George W. Bush administration, commented that the Trump family will become the first presidential family in history to profit excessively from war. "These (Gulf) countries are under immense pressure to buy weapons from the president's sons so that the president will act according to their wishes." Powerus's expansion is inseparable from the support of Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump, both of whom are significant shareholders in Powerus. Neither Donald Trump Jr. nor Eric Trump have responded to reports of Powerus marketing drone interceptors to the Middle East. Since Trump's return to the White House, Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump have aggressively expanded the Trump family's business empire, with ventures including cryptocurrency, military industry, rocket components, market forecasting, and rare earth magnets. According to an Associated Press report on March 12, Powerus previously won a government drone tender worth up to $1.1 billion. By becoming shareholders in Powerus, Trump's two sons could potentially reap substantial profits. When asked about potential nepotism in the bidding process, Eric Trump stated, "I'm very proud to be able to invest in companies I approve of. Drones are the future." Veljkovic added that the Trump sons' investment was unrelated to the company's winning bid, stating, "Our company's focus is not on politics." Powerus was founded in 2025. According to a previous Reuters report, on March 9th, with the support of Trump's two sons, Powerus merged with Aureus Greenway Holdings, a publicly traded company primarily operating golf courses, successfully achieving a backdoor listing. Aureus Greenway Holdings also has close ties to Trump's two sons.

data.title: The Next Generation Internet – AI, Blockchain, and the Machine Economy
data.description:
When AI no longer merely answers questions but begins to act on behalf of humans, a fundamental question arises: How can machines pay for, authorize, and leave a credible record of their actions in the real world? Over the past two years, discussions about the combination of AI and blockchain have finally shifted from the narrative of "leveraging" to the pragmatic construction of "infrastructure coupling." As outlined by a16z last year, the intersection of the two is forming a new internet architecture around identity, data, payment, ownership, and collaboration. By 2026, the market's focus will have completely shifted from "what generative AI can do" to "how AI agents participate in real economic activities." This means that the role of blockchain is undergoing a silent but profound transformation: it is evolving from a synonym for crypto assets and speculation into a settlement layer, identity layer, authorization layer, and audit layer in the era of AI agents. Only when AI truly begins to "act" will blockchain transform from a secondary technology into a necessity-based infrastructure. Three major new changes: a16z's views last year were like a precise "map," while the industry development over the past year has been like building roads on that map. The most significant changes are threefold: 1. AI Agents Become the Main Narrative By 2026, AI agents have transformed from a concept into a core industry element. For example, Base, in its 2026 roadmap, explicitly prioritizes the "AI agent economy," aiming to enable agents to perform actions with money through smart accounts, payment protocols, and more. This signifies that the industry has entered a new phase where AI acts on behalf of others: once an agent possesses executive power, it requires wallets, permissions, and identity—precisely where the inherent advantages of blockchain and stablecoins lie. AI agents may become the core driver of the next wave of on-chain interaction growth. 2. "Verifiability" Becomes a Central Theme With the proliferation of AI-generated content, market demand for traceability of source, authenticity, and responsibility has surged. Institutions are facing pressure to prove data origins, model decisions, and attribution of responsibility. Blockchain's immutable records, timestamps, and audit trails are being seen as key underlying capabilities to meet these "decision traceability" requirements. Future competition will not only be about computing power, but also about the ability to create trustworthy records. 3. Identity and Permissions Take Precedence Over Computing Power Unlike the earlier hot topic of "decentralized computing power," this year's more realistic focus is on identity, permissions, reputation, and authorization. a16z emphasized in its discussions earlier this year that blockchain can support persistent user context for AI agents, portable identities, and on-chain payments without intermediaries. The most likely products to mature in the short term are those that allow AI agents to have restricted wallets, allow users to authorize AI to use specific data, and allow enterprises to audit the complete action chain of AI. Based on the above changes, the integration of AI and blockchain is focusing on four distinct intersections: AI-assisted payments: This is currently the direction with the greatest commercial potential. Stablecoins and on-chain payments are naturally suited for small-amount, high-frequency, automated machine-to-machine (M2M) transactions. AI agents can automatically purchase APIs, subscribe to services, or execute payments. This is transforming the crypto economy from an "asset system" into a "payment layer for the machine economy." Verifiable AI: The goal is not to make AI smarter, but to make it more trustworthy. It focuses on model version, input data source, whether the output has been tampered with, and whether the execution process can be audited. The value of blockchain lies in providing traceable and verifiable records for critical decision-making events, which is crucial for high-risk areas such as finance, healthcare, and compliance. User-Controlled Data and Context: The more intelligent AI becomes, the more it needs continuous user context. Blockchain and related protocols allow users to carry their authorizations, credentials, and historical records between different services, achieving data portability and sovereignty. This will give rise to new data business models: users can authorize, price, and audit their own data and behavior. Trustworthy Content and Traceability: In an era of AI-generated content overload, the authenticity of information is difficult to verify. Blockchain can serve as a "proof layer," recording the creation, editing, and signing history of content, providing a foundation for verifying the authenticity of media, copyright, and even corporate documents, and combating the risk of systemic information pollution. The historic convergence of two technology curves is a critical window of opportunity because AI and blockchain are simultaneously entering a phase of infrastructure restructuring: AI is evolving from a tool to an agent, while crypto is shifting from a speculative asset to a payment, identity, and settlement infrastructure. The convergence of these two technology curves in 2026 will give rise to a new narrative that "machines also need economic identity." In the next 1-3 years, AI & Blockchain may advance along the following timeline: Short term (1-2 years): Payment and identity will mature first. This is the direction closest to commercial value and with the least technological friction; enterprises will prioritize solving API payments, subscription settlements, and permission verification for agents. Mid-term (2-3 years): Verifiable workflows will become standard capabilities. The complete AI workflow—from data input and model invocation to output—will universally require on-chain audit traces. Long-term (3+ years): A true machine economy/agent economy may emerge. AI agents will be able to autonomously trade services, purchase data, and collaborate to complete tasks, while blockchain will become the universal language for negotiation, settlement, and trust building between machines. The opportunities within this are clearly visible: stablecoin-driven AI agent payments, on-chain identity and authorization, verifiable AI services for high-value markets, and content traceability to combat information pollution all hold enormous potential. However, risks should not be ignored: Overly enthusiastic narratives coupled with insufficient practical application may create a bubble; the allocation of responsibility for errors made by AI agents remains a legal blind spot; user experience barriers for wallets, keys, etc., remain high; furthermore, centralized AI platforms may internalize "trusted agent payment" as a platform function, thereby squeezing the development space for open protocols.

Conclusion

If we consider a16z's article from last year as a "map of AI x blockchain," then the progress in 2026 shows that the real battleground is no longer "whether AI is on the blockchain," but rather who can provide the entire infrastructure stack of identity, payment, authorization, auditing, and trusted records once AI agents enter the real economy. In short: The ultimate significance of blockchain for AI is not to turn AI into on-chain applications, but to enable AI to safely enter and transform the real world in a trustworthy, controllable, and verifiable manner. The dawn of the machine economy may have already quietly begun with lines of smart contract code and countless automated payments.
